item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, my friend. Kind words mean alot on this one!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember reading this when it was in the draft stage, and I'm happy to see it now on the mainlist. It's very good, and very heartfelt.

I said it before, I'll say it again. Well done.

+1

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: thanks for reading and the note, I'll change that

I was sort of confused, but I liked it. Especially because it’s the first SCP I’ve read that takes place in my home country, and so close to my summer home!

I give it a 1+ for this and the dread of the trapped MTF captain.

Corrections: The city of “Bodrum” is spelt wrong and the Turkish phrase is in Greek. :>). Turkish translation would be “Şehirin Ruhu” roughly.

item.title: Addendums
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed reading this, as others commented, this article has a sense of sadness for places that are no more… I liked that the anomaly is not malevolent, nor wants to do harm. It just wants to be visited!

Also, nice way to explain the discovery of the anomaly, it is not so easy to do this for an anomaly that happens only in some circumstances!

The reading was somewhat strange, because the "addendum" box style didn't look like to be clicked, so I just continued reading. After reaching the end of the article, and returned to read the ConProc section, it made sense to me, I thought I missed the mention of Cap. Waltham. After going all the way down again, I saw in the footnotes that here was some entries that I didn't see in the text! So I clicked the addendums, to finally understand the missing pieces…
What a deeply thoughtful piece. Thank you for posting this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey hey, thats a speck in my eye, and I keep pressing that +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Waltham's arc feels a little rushed and the -1 entity pretty much just dumped exposition when I was expecting it to behave cryptically as the rest of the article would have built me up to believe.

I have edited the end of the log to better indicate the reasoning on Waltham's part and also to maintain the tone of the entity as throughout (which is obtuse and childlike)

Hope that makes a difference in your eyes. Either way, appreciate the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for the lonely, lovable loci lad

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I think you’re right, the inclusion of the village and -B in the description sorta deflates the discovery later on.

I've made that change, including the information about -A only once Rossi's experience has occurred and the Foundation has figured out there is a real town. I think it improves the pacing a lot.

Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find myself in the same place as Nico here, in that it's a competently written work that isn't quite satisfactory to me, though some of the sentence structuring threw me off a few more times than I would have liked. Obviously, that's a personal gripe, but it is what it is.

It feels like the parts that are stretched are all within setting the atmosphere, which while I appreciate, leaves the last log feeling a bit like retreading ground. I feel like a way to remedy that would be to have introduced -A later than within the initial phases of the description. I think having that come out later would allow for a more natural flow of establishing that the place is real, has a history of abandonment, and a reason to want more people to come through would pull the pieces a little tighter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Competently written, though I feel it ends a little bit suddenly. I was wondering when the hook established in the containment procedures was going to kick in and then it came and went so fast I almost missed it. Waltham's arc feels a little rushed and the -1 entity pretty much just dumped exposition when I was expecting it to behave cryptically as the rest of the article would have built me up to believe. In short, a little more would have gone a long way here.

Make no mistake, what is here is good, I just wish there were a little more of it to really complete it.

item.title: Re: When will he miss?
item.content:encoded: You keep leaving the comments, I'll keep enjoying them, its like a whole ecosystem of appreciation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given your wonderfully beautiful singing and art, this is an especially lovely compliment. Thank you <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A great example of getting your point across with crystal clarity, without force-feeding it. Moody, atmospheric, sad, and even a bit relatable. Excellent stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Among your works, this one was exceptionally beautiful. Definitely hit close to home as well… +1

item.title: When will he miss?
item.content:encoded:
How many comments can I leave like this before it just becomes implied with my +1? I love this piece so much. Like a child hugging a teddy bear until the stitches burst, this evokes such amazing imagery of loneliness and the things we do to to rebuild communities and relationships we have lost.

Another phenomenal entry to the Karpin archive.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This is an incredibly personal piece. It starts off one way, leading you hopefully to expect one thing and then ending a completely different way in terms of what sort of story you were reading.

I hope its effective, it means a lot to me.

Critters: HarryBlank, Pedagon, Ralliston, Quadraginta, LordStonefish, Marcelles_Raynes, Elunerazim, & Dysadron
